title: Memory Decay in Vanilla RNNs
concept: Because vanilla RNNs overwrite their hidden state at every single step, early context gets diluted and lost as fresh words are processed.

exercises.prompt: Why do vanilla RNNs have trouble remembering things over long sequences?

options.text: They have too many memory storage compartments.
They overwrite their memory state at every single step.
They only read the last word of a sentence.

exercises.explain: Vanilla Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs) struggle with long-term memory because their simple hidden state gets updated and overwritten at every step.

cards.title: Fading Memories
cards.body: Have you ever read a long sentence and completely forgotten how it started by the time you reached the period? Vanilla RNNs do this all the time! Let's find out why their memories fade so fast.

cards.title: What is Memory Decay?
cards.body: Memory decay happens in vanilla RNNs because they have a single, limited hidden state. At every single step, the network mixes the incoming word with its current state to write a new one. Since there is no protected memory slot, old information gets diluted and quickly lost.

cards.title: The Whisper Game
cards.body: Imagine passing a secret whisper down a long line of 50 people. Every time someone whispers it to the next person, they also add a new detail of their own. By the time it reaches the end, the original message is completely overwritten and lost! That's exactly how a vanilla RNN handles old information.

cards.title: Lost in the Middle
cards.body: If you feed an RNN the sentence: "The girl who won the science fair, played soccer, read three books, and ran a marathon, was happy." \n\nBy the time the RNN reaches "was happy", it has updated its memory so many times for the middle words that it has forgotten the subject was "girl" (singular) rather than "books" (plural)!

cards.title: Not a Choice, a Limitation
cards.body: It is a myth that RNNs just choose to ignore early words because they are unimportant. In reality, the network's math forces it to constantly overwrite its past. It wants to remember, but its simple architecture physically lacks the space to keep old and new data separated!

cards.title: Memory Bottleneck
cards.body: Look at how memory behaves in a simple RNN compared to how we want it to behave. When the state is updated, old context is pushed out.

left.text: One small cup. Adding new water (words) overflows the cup, spilling and losing the old water.

right.text: Multiple drawers where old information can be safely locked away while new files are added.

exercises.prompt: What causes early information to get lost in a vanilla RNN?

options.text: The model completely stops processing new words after step ten.
The hidden state is continuously overwritten with every incoming word.
The text is automatically deleted from the computer's hard drive.
It stores older words in a separate database that it forgets to open.

exercises.explain: Vanilla RNNs do not have gates to protect old information. They mix everything into one state at every step, so old information quickly washes away.

exercises.prompt: Vanilla RNNs struggle to remember information from the beginning of long paragraphs.
exercises.explain: True! Because the hidden state is updated at every single word, a vanilla RNN has a very hard time keeping track of details from the beginning of a long text.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the missing word to explain how RNN memory fades.
exercises.sentence: Because vanilla RNNs update their single hidden state at every step, early context gets overwritten by new information.
exercises.explain: Vanilla RNNs do not have separate memory zones, so older context gets overwritten and diluted by every new word that enters the network.

exercises.prompt: An AI using a vanilla RNN and a human-like model both try to finish a long sentence. Which system shows the classic grammatical error caused by RNN memory decay?

options.text: System A: "The boy who sat near the window, read many heavy books, and ate lunch, were tired."
System B: "The boy who sat near the window, read many heavy books, and ate lunch, was tired."

exercises.explain: System A is the AI because vanilla RNNs suffer from memory decay and struggle to link early words (like "boy") to verbs at the very end of a long sentence. System B correctly retains the context.

exercises.prompt: Why do vanilla RNNs struggle to remember the beginning of a long book?

options.text: It overwrites its memory at every step, diluting old details with new words.
It saves each word in a separate, permanent memory folder.
It ignores new words and only remembers the very first word.
It uses a mathematical backup drive to restore old state histories.

exercises.explain: Vanilla RNNs use a single, continuous hidden state update that overwrites its contents at every step. This makes it mathematically and practically impossible to keep early memories intact over long sequences without them getting completely washed out by new words.
